channel.title: Nature Physics
channel.description: Nature Physics offers news and reviews alongside top-quality research papers in a monthly publication, covering the entire spectrum of physics. Physics addresses the properties and interactions of matter and energy, and plays a key role in the development of a broad range of technologies. To reflect this, Nature Physics covers all areas of pure and applied physics research. The journal focuses on core physics disciplines, but is also open to a broad range of topics whose central theme falls within the bounds of physics.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Exceptional deficiency of non-Hermitian systems
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03259-7

Exceptional points and their related phenomena are limited to a narrow bandwidth and require precise control to realize. Now a high-dimensional generalization of exceptional points is shown to enable broadband non-Hermitian dynamics.
item.dc:title: Exceptional deficiency of non-Hermitian systems
item.dc:creator: Zhen Li, Rundong Cai, Xulong Wang, et al.; last authors: Masatoshi Sato, Guancong Ma
Rundong Cai
Xulong Wang
Kenji Shimomura

Zhesen Yang
Masatoshi Sato
Guancong Ma

Authors: Zhen Li, Rundong Cai, Xulong Wang, et al.; last authors: Masatoshi Sato, Guancong Ma

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03259-7 Exceptional points and their related phenomena are limited to a narrow bandwidth and require precise control to realize. Now a high-dimensional generalization of exceptional points is shown to enable broadband non-Hermitian dynamics.

item.title: Planckian scattering and parallel conduction channels in an iron chalcogenide superconductor
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03266-8

The link between the strange metal with its linear-in-temperature resistivity and superconductivity is ambiguous. Now, a channel in the normal state whose scattering rate is linear in temperature is shown to drive superconductivity in FeTe1−xSex.
item.dc:title: Planckian scattering and parallel conduction channels in an iron chalcogenide superconductor
item.dc:creator: Ralph Romero III, Hee Taek Yi, Seongshik Oh, N. P. Armitage
Hee Taek Yi
Seongshik Oh
N. P. Armitage
item.description:
Authors: Ralph Romero III, Hee Taek Yi, Seongshik Oh, N. P. Armitage

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Seongshik Oh, N. P. Armitage

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Tags: #superconduct

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03266-8 The link between the strange metal with its linear-in-temperature resistivity and superconductivity is ambiguous. Now, a channel in the normal state whose scattering rate is linear in temperature is shown to drive superconductivity in FeTe1−xSex.

item.title: Field-induced superconductivity in a magnetically doped two-dimensional crystal
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03272-w

Magnetic fields usually weaken superconductivity. By contrast, a material platform is demonstrated where applying a moderate field induces superconductivity.
item.dc:title: Field-induced superconductivity in a magnetically doped two-dimensional crystal
item.dc:creator: Adrian Llanos, Veronica Show, Reiley Dorrian, Joseph Falson
Veronica Show
Reiley Dorrian
Joseph Falson

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Tags: #superconduct

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03272-w Magnetic fields usually weaken superconductivity. By contrast, a material platform is demonstrated where applying a moderate field induces superconductivity.

item.title: Laser-induced nucleation of magnetic hopfions
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 21 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03236-0

The creation of stable and isolated magnetic hopfions—three-dimensional topological solitons—has remained experimentally challenging. Now the laser-induced nucleation of hopfions has been achieved in a chiral magnet.
item.dc:title: Laser-induced nucleation of magnetic hopfions
item.dc:creator: Xiaowen Chen, Donghai Yang, Zefang Li, et al.; last authors: Xuewen Fu, Fengshan Zheng

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 21 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03236-0 The creation of stable and isolated magnetic hopfions—three-dimensional topological solitons—has remained experimentally challenging. Now the laser-induced nucleation of hopfions has been achieved in a chiral magnet.

item.title: Two-electron quantum walks for probing entanglement and decoherence in an electron microscope
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 21 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03254-y

Entanglement between particles offers insights into quantum behaviour, but methods for studying it in free-electron systems are lacking. Now a two-electron quantum walk is used to probe decoherence of free electrons inside an electron microscope.
item.dc:title: Two-electron quantum walks for probing entanglement and decoherence in an electron microscope
item.dc:creator: Offek Tziperman, David Nabben, Ron Ruimy, et al.; last authors: Ido Kaminer, Peter Baum

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 21 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03254-y Entanglement between particles offers insights into quantum behaviour, but methods for studying it in free-electron systems are lacking. Now a two-electron quantum walk is used to probe decoherence of free electrons inside an electron microscope.

item.title: Optical excitations reshape the spin-wave spectrum in antiferromagnets
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03231-5

Charge-transfer excitations, which define the optical bandgap in many insulators, also contribute to magnetic exchange in antiferromagnets. Femtosecond optical pumping of these transitions in canted antiferromagnet DyFeO3 reshapes the spin-wave spectrum — the set of collective spin excitations that define the dynamics of the antiferromagnet — without destroying the long-range order.
item.dc:title: Optical excitations reshape the spin-wave spectrum in antiferromagnets
item.description:
Authors: Authors not available in source RSS

Last/corresponding-author proxy: not available

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini failed; kept to avoid false negative

Tags: #ferromagnet #antiferromagnet

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03231-5 Charge-transfer excitations, which define the optical bandgap in many insulators, also contribute to magnetic exchange in antiferromagnets. Femtosecond optical pumping of these transitions in canted antiferromagnet DyFeO3 reshapes the spin-wave spectrum — the set of collective spin excitations that define the dynamics of the antiferromagnet — without destroying the long-range order.

item.title: Average topological phase in a disordered Rydberg atom array
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03271-x

In addition to strongly protected topological phases that rely on exact symmetries, theory predicts that disorder can stabilize weakly protected phases in mixed quantum states, and an example of the latter is now observed in a Rydberg atom array.
item.dc:title: Average topological phase in a disordered Rydberg atom array
item.dc:creator: Zongpei Yue, Yu-Feng Mao, Xinhui Liang, et al.; last authors: Yong Xu, Li You

item.title: Transverse optical torque observed at the nanoscale
item.content:encoded:
Nature Physics, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03268-6

Optical forces and torques on nanoparticles are difficult to measure due to the diffraction limit of light. Now, transverse optical torque is observed through the optical trapping and spatial tracking of a designed microscale structure.
item.dc:title: Transverse optical torque observed at the nanoscale
item.dc:creator: Ryoma Fukuhara, Tsutomu Shimura, Yoshito Y. Tanaka
Tsutomu Shimura
Yoshito Y. Tanaka

Abstract: Nature Physics, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03268-6 Optical forces and torques on nanoparticles are difficult to measure due to the diffraction limit of light. Now, transverse optical torque is observed through the optical trapping and spatial tracking of a designed microscale structure.
